sourceQuestion:
Consider three processes, all arriving at time zero, with total execution time of 10, 20, and 30 units, respectively. Each process spends the first 20% of execution time doing I/O, the next 60% of time doing computation, and the last 20% of time doing I/O again. The operating system uses a shortest remaining compute time first scheduling algorithm and schedules a new process either when the running process gets blocked on I/O or when the running process finishes its compute burst. Assume that all I/O operations can be overlapped as much as possible. For what percentage of time does the CPU remain idle?

A. 0%
B. 10.6%
C. 30.0%
D. 89.4%

parsed.stem: Consider three processes, all arriving at time zero, with total execution time of 10, 20, and 30 units, respectively. Each process spends the first 20% of execution time doing I/O, the next 60% of time doing computation, and the last 20% of time doing I/O again. The operating system uses a shortest remaining compute time first scheduling algorithm and schedules a new process either when the running process gets blocked on I/O or when the running process finishes its compute burst. Assume that all I/O operations can be overlapped as much as possible. For what percentage of time does the CPU remain idle?

sourceQuestion:
Group 1 contains some CPU scheduling algorithms and Group 2 contains some applications. Match entries in Group 1 to entries in Group 2.

Group I:
(P) Gang Scheduling
(Q) Rate Monotonic Scheduling
(R) Fair Share Scheduling

Group II:
(1) Guaranteed Scheduling
(2) Real-time Scheduling
(3) Thread Scheduling

A. P-3, Q-2, R-1
B. P-1, Q-2, R-3
C. P-2, Q-3, R-1
D. P-1, Q-3, R-2

parsed.stem:
Group 1 contains some CPU scheduling algorithms and Group 2 contains some applications. Match entries in Group 1 to entries in Group 2.

(P) Gang Scheduling
(Q) Rate Monotonic Scheduling
(R) Fair Share Scheduling

Group II:
(1) Guaranteed Scheduling
(2) Real-time Scheduling
(3) Thread Scheduling

sourceQuestion:
An operating system uses Shortest Remaining Time first (SRT) process scheduling algorithm. Consider the arrival times and execution times for the following processes:

Process | Execution Time | Arrival Time
P1      | 20             | 0
P2      | 25             | 15
P3      | 10             | 30
P4      | 15             | 45

What is the total waiting time for process P2?

A. 5
B. 15
C. 40
D. 55

parsed.stem:
An operating system uses Shortest Remaining Time first (SRT) process scheduling algorithm. Consider the arrival times and execution times for the following processes:

Process | Execution Time | Arrival Time
P1      | 20             | 0
P2      | 25             | 15
P3      | 10             | 30
P4      | 15             | 45

What is the total waiting time for process P2?

sourceQuestion:
In the following process state transition diagram for a uniprocessor system, assume that there are always some processes in the ready state:

(Diagram shows states: Start -> Ready <-> Running -> Terminated, Running -> Blocked -> Ready)
Transitions: A (Start->Ready), B (Ready->Running), C (Running->Ready), D (Running->Blocked), E (Blocked->Ready), F (Running->Terminated)

Now consider the following statements:
I. If a process makes a transition D, it would result in another process making transition B immediately.
II. A process P2 in blocked state can make transition E while another process P3 is running.
III. The OS uses preemptive scheduling.
IV. The OS uses non-preemptive scheduling.

Which of the above statements are TRUE?

A. I and II
B. I and III
C. II and III
D. II and IV

parsed.stem:
In the following process state transition diagram for a uniprocessor system, assume that there are always some processes in the ready state:

(Diagram shows states: Start -> Ready <-> Running -> Terminated, Running -> Blocked -> Ready)
Transitions: A (Start->Ready), B (Ready->Running), C (Running->Ready), D (Running->Blocked), E (Blocked->Ready), F (Running->Terminated)

Now consider the following statements:
I. If a process makes a transition D, it would result in another process making transition B immediately.
II. A process P2 in blocked state can make transition E while another process P3 is running.
III. The OS uses preemptive scheduling.
IV. The OS uses non-preemptive scheduling.

Which of the above statements are TRUE?

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following statements are TRUE?
I. Shortest remaining time first scheduling may cause starvation
II. Preemptive scheduling may cause starvation
III. Round robin is better than FCFS in terms of response time

A. I only
B. I and III only
C. II and III only
D. I, II and III

parsed.stem:
Which of the following statements are TRUE?
I. Shortest remaining time first scheduling may cause starvation
II. Preemptive scheduling may cause starvation
III. Round robin is better than FCFS in terms of response time

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following table of arrival time and burst time for three processes P0, P1 and P2.

Process | Arrival Time | Burst Time
P0      |     0 ms     |    9 ms
P1      |     1 ms     |    4 ms
P2      |     2 ms     |    9 ms

The pre-emptive shortest job first scheduling algorithm is used. Scheduling is carried out only at arrival or completion of processes. What is the average waiting time for the three processes?

A. 5.0 ms
B. 4.33 ms
C. 6.33 ms
D. 7.33 ms

parsed.stem:
Consider the following table of arrival time and burst time for three processes P0, P1 and P2.

Process | Arrival Time | Burst Time
P0      |     0 ms     |    9 ms
P1      |     1 ms     |    4 ms
P2      |     2 ms     |    9 ms

The pre-emptive shortest job first scheduling algorithm is used. Scheduling is carried out only at arrival or completion of processes. What is the average waiting time for the three processes?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the 3 processes, P1, P2 and P3 shown in the table.

Process | Arrival Time | Time Units Required
P1      |      0       |         3
P2      |      1       |         6
P3      |      4       |         4

The completion order of the 3 processes under the policies FCFS and RR2 (round robin scheduling with CPU quantum of 2 time units) are:

A. FCFS: P1, P2, P3    RR2: P1, P3, P2
B. FCFS: P1, P3, P2    RR2: P1, P3, P2
C. FCFS: P1, P2, P3    RR2: P1, P2, P3
D. FCFS: P1, P3, P2    RR2: P1, P2, P3
sourceAnswer: A. FCFS: P1, P2, P3    RR2: P1, P3, P2

parsed.stem:
Consider the 3 processes, P1, P2 and P3 shown in the table.

Process | Arrival Time | Time Units Required
P1      |      0       |         3
P2      |      1       |         6
P3      |      4       |         4

The completion order of the 3 processes under the policies FCFS and RR2 (round robin scheduling with CPU quantum of 2 time units) are:

options.text: FCFS: P1, P2, P3 RR2: P1, P3, P2
FCFS: P1, P3, P2 RR2: P1, P3, P2
FCFS: P1, P2, P3 RR2: P1, P2, P3
FCFS: P1, P3, P2 RR2: P1, P2, P3

sourceQuestion:
A scheduling algorithm assigns priority proportional to the waiting time of a process. Every process starts with priority zero (the lowest priority). The scheduler re-evaluates the process priorities every T time units and decides the next process to schedule. Which one of the following is TRUE if the processes have no I/O operations and all arrive at time zero?

A. This algorithm is equivalent to the first-come first-served algorithm.
B. This algorithm is equivalent to the round-robin algorithm.
C. This algorithm is equivalent to the shortest-job-first algorithm.
D. This algorithm is equivalent to the shortest-remaining-time-first algorithm.
sourceAnswer: B. This algorithm is equivalent to the round-robin algorithm.

parsed.stem: A scheduling algorithm assigns priority proportional to the waiting time of a process. Every process starts with priority zero (the lowest priority). The scheduler re-evaluates the process priorities every T time units and decides the next process to schedule. Which one of the following is TRUE if the processes have no I/O operations and all arrive at time zero?

options.text: This algorithm is equivalent to the first-come first-served algorithm.
This algorithm is equivalent to the round-robin algorithm.
This algorithm is equivalent to the shortest-job-first algorithm.
This algorithm is equivalent to the shortest-remaining-time-first algorithm.

sourceQuestion:
The maximum number of processes that can be in Running state for a computer system with n CPUs is:
A. n
B. n^2
C. 2^n
D. Independent of n

parsed.stem: The maximum number of processes that can be in Running state for a computer system with n CPUs is:

sourceQuestion:
For the processes listed in the following table, which of the following scheduling schemes will give the lowest average turnaround time?

Process | Arrival Time | Process Time
   A    |      0       |      3
   B    |      1       |      6
   C    |      3       |      2
   D    |      6       |      2

A. First Come First Serve
B. Non-preemptive Shortest Job First
C. Shortest Remaining Time
D. Round Robin with Quantum value 2

sourceQuestion:
Consider an arbitrary set of CPU-bound processes with unequal CPU burst lengths submitted at the same time to a computer system. Which one of the following process scheduling algorithms would minimize the average waiting time in the ready queue?
A. Shortest remaining time first
B. Round-robin with a time quantum less than the shortest CPU burst
C. Uniform random
D. Highest priority first with each process assigned a random priority

parsed.stem: Consider an arbitrary set of CPU-bound processes with unequal CPU burst lengths submitted at the same time to a computer system. Which one of the following process scheduling algorithms would minimize the average waiting time in the ready queue?

options.text: Round-robin with a time quantum less than the shortest CPU burst
Highest priority first with each process assigned a random priority

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following statements about process state transitions for a system using preemptive scheduling.
I. A running process can move to ready state.
II. A ready process can move to running state.
III. A blocked process can move to running state.
IV. A blocked process can move to ready state.
Which of the above statements are TRUE?
A. I, II, and III only
B. II and III only
C. I, II, and IV only
D. I, II, III, and IV only
sourceAnswer: C. I, II, and IV only

parsed.stem:
Consider the following statements about process state transitions for a system using preemptive scheduling.
I. A running process can move to ready state.
II. A ready process can move to running state.
III. A blocked process can move to running state.
IV. A blocked process can move to ready state.
Which of the above statements are TRUE?

options.text: I, II, III, and IV only

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following statement(s) is/are correct in the context of process scheduling?
A. Turnaround time includes waiting time
B. The goal is to only maximize utilization and minimize throughput
C. Round-robin policy can be used even when the time required by each of the processes is not known apriori
D. Implementing preemptive scheduling needs hardware support

parsed.stem: Which of the following statement(s) is/are correct in the context of process scheduling?

options.text: The goal is to only maximize utilization and minimize throughput
Round-robin policy can be used even when the time required by each of the processes is not known apriori
Implementing preemptive scheduling needs hardware support

sourceQuestion:
Consider four processes P, Q, R, and S scheduled on a CPU as per round robin algorithm with a time quantum of 4. The processes arrive in the order P, Q, R, S, all at time 0. There is exactly one context switch from P to Q, exactly one context switch from Q to R, and exactly two context switches from Q to S. There is no context switch from S to P. Switching from X to Y means X stops executing and Y starts executing.
Which one of the following is NOT possible as the CPU burst time (in time units) of the processes?
A. P = 2, Q = 10, R = 3, S = 4
B. P = 2, Q = 6, R = 3, S = 5
C. P = 2, Q = 10, R = 3, S = 8
D. P = 2, Q = 10, R = 3, S = 12

parsed.stem:
Consider four processes P, Q, R, and S scheduled on a CPU as per round robin algorithm with a time quantum of 4. The processes arrive in the order P, Q, R, S, all at time 0. There is exactly one context switch from P to Q, exactly one context switch from Q to R, and exactly two context switches from Q to S. There is no context switch from S to P. Switching from X to Y means X stops executing and Y starts executing.
Which one of the following is NOT possible as the CPU burst time (in time units) of the processes?

Which one or more of the following CPU scheduling algorithms can potentially cause starvation?
A. First-in First-Out
B. Round Robin
C. Priority Scheduling
D. Shortest Job First

A. Running to Ready
B. Waiting to Running
C. Ready to Waiting
D. Running to Terminated

sourceQuestion:
Consider a single processor system with four processes A, B, C, D, represented as given below, where for each process the first value is its arrival time, and the second value is its burst time.
A(0, 10), B(2, 6), C(4, 3), D(6, 7)
Which one of the following options gives the average waiting times when preemptive Shortest Remaining Time First (SRTF) CPU and Non-Preemptive Shortest Job First (NP-SJF) CPU scheduling algorithms are used?
A. SRTF = 6, NP-SJF = 7
B. SRTF = 6, NP-SJF = 7.5
C. SRTF = 7, NP-SJF = 7.5
D. SRTF = 7, NP-SJF = 8.5

A(0, 10), B(2, 6), C(4, 3), D(6, 7)
Which one of the following options gives the average waiting times when preemptive Shortest Remaining Time First (SRTF) CPU and Non-Preemptive Shortest Job First (NP-SJF) CPU scheduling algorithms are used?

sourceQuestion:
A computer has two processors, M1 and M2. Four processes with CPU bursts of 20, 16, 25, and 10 milliseconds, respectively, arrive at the same time and these are the only processes in the system. The scheduler uses non-preemptive priority scheduling, with priorities decided as follows:
- M1 uses priority of execution for the processes as P1 > P2 > P3 > P4, i.e., P1 and P4 have highest and lowest priorities, respectively.
- M2 uses priority of execution for the processes as P4 > P3 > P2 > P1 (without violating highest priority rule), i.e., P1 and P4 have highest and lowest priorities, respectively.
A process is scheduled to a processor if the processor is free and no other process with a higher priority is waiting with that processor. Assume each process has its own I/O to the processor to its own CPU burst.
What is the average waiting time of the processes in milliseconds?
A. 9.00
B. 8.75
C. 6.50
D. 7.50

parsed.stem:
A computer has two processors, M1 and M2. Four processes with CPU bursts of 20, 16, 25, and 10 milliseconds, respectively, arrive at the same time and these are the only processes in the system. The scheduler uses non-preemptive priority scheduling, with priorities decided as follows:
- M1 uses priority of execution for the processes as P1 > P2 > P3 > P4, i.e., P1 and P4 have highest and lowest priorities, respectively.
- M2 uses priority of execution for the processes as P4 > P3 > P2 > P1 (without violating highest priority rule), i.e., P1 and P4 have highest and lowest priorities, respectively.
A process is scheduled to a processor if the processor is free and no other process with a higher priority is waiting with that processor. Assume each process has its own I/O to the processor to its own CPU burst.
What is the average waiting time of the processes in milliseconds?

sourceQuestion:
We wish to schedule three processes P1, P2 and P3 on a uniprocessor system. The priorities, CPU time requirements and arrival times of the processes are as shown below.

Process | Priority      | CPU time required | Arrival time
P1      | 10 (highest)  | 9 sec             | 0:00:00
P2      | 2             | 10 sec            | 0:00:05
P3      | 3 (lowest)    | 15 sec            | 0:00:03

We have a choice of preemptive or non-preemptive scheduling. In preemptive scheduling, a late-arriving higher priority process can preempt the currently executing process. In non-preemptive scheduling, once a process starts, it runs to completion.

What are the turnaround times of P2 using preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling respectively?

A. 30 sec; 30 sec
B. 30 sec; 10 sec
C. 30 sec; 42 sec
D. 30 sec; 42 sec

parsed.stem:
We wish to schedule three processes P1, P2 and P3 on a uniprocessor system. The priorities, CPU time requirements and arrival times of the processes are as shown below.

Process | Priority      | CPU time required | Arrival time
P1      | 10 (highest)  | 9 sec             | 0:00:00
P2      | 2             | 10 sec            | 0:00:05
P3      | 3 (lowest)    | 15 sec            | 0:00:03

We have a choice of preemptive or non-preemptive scheduling. In preemptive scheduling, a late-arriving higher priority process can preempt the currently executing process. In non-preemptive scheduling, once a process starts, it runs to completion.

What are the turnaround times of P2 using preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling respectively?

sourceQuestion:
In the working-set strategy, which of the following is done by the operating system to prevent thrashing?

I. It initiates another process if there are enough extra frames.
II. It selects a process to suspend if the sum of the sizes of the working-sets exceeds the total number of available frames.

A. Only I
B. Only II
C. Neither I nor II
D. Both I and II

parsed.stem:
In the working-set strategy, which of the following is done by the operating system to prevent thrashing?

I. It initiates another process if there are enough extra frames.
II. It selects a process to suspend if the sum of the sizes of the working-sets exceeds the total number of available frames.

sourceQuestion:
The arrival time, priority, and duration of the CPU and I/O bursts for each of three processes P1, P2 and P3 are given in the table below. Each process has a CPU burst followed by an I/O burst followed by another CPU burst. Assume that each process has its own I/O resource.

Process | Arrival Time | Priority   | Burst duration (CPU) | Burst duration (I/O) | Burst duration (CPU)
P1      | 0            | 2          | 5                    | 3                    | 2
P2      | 1            | 3 (lowest) | 2                    | 2                    | 6
P3      | 3            | 1 (highest)| 2                    | 2                    | 1

The multi-programmed operating system uses preemptive priority scheduling. What are the finish times of processes P1, P2 and P3?

A. 12, 15, 9
B. 10, 15, 9
C. 11, 16, 10
D. 12, 17, 11

parsed.stem:
The arrival time, priority, and duration of the CPU and I/O bursts for each of three processes P1, P2 and P3 are given in the table below. Each process has a CPU burst followed by an I/O burst followed by another CPU burst. Assume that each process has its own I/O resource.

Process | Arrival Time | Priority   | Burst duration (CPU) | Burst duration (I/O) | Burst duration (CPU)
P1      | 0            | 2          | 5                    | 3                    | 2
P2      | 1            | 3 (lowest) | 2                    | 2                    | 6
P3      | 3            | 1 (highest)| 2                    | 2                    | 1

The multi-programmed operating system uses preemptive priority scheduling. What are the finish times of processes P1, P2 and P3?

sourceQuestion:
Consider n jobs J_1, J_2, ..., J_n such that job J_i has execution time t_i and a non-negative integer weight w_i. The weighted mean completion time of the jobs is defined to be sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i * C_i, where C_i is the completion time of job J_i. Assuming that there is only one processor available, in what order must the jobs be executed in order to minimize sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i * C_i?

A. Non-decreasing order of t_i
B. Non-increasing order of w_i
C. Non-increasing order of w_i/t_i
D. Non-increasing order of w_i * t_i

parsed.stem: Consider n jobs J_1, J_2, ..., J_n such that job J_i has execution time t_i and a non-negative integer weight w_i. The weighted mean completion time of the jobs is defined to be sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i * C_i, where C_i is the completion time of job J_i. Assuming that there is only one processor available, in what order must the jobs be executed in order to minimize sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i * C_i?

sourceQuestion:
If the time-slice used in the round-robin scheduling policy is more than the maximum time required to execute any process, then the policy will

A. degenerate to shortest job first
B. degenerate to priority scheduling
C. degenerate to first come first serve
D. none of the above
sourceAnswer: C. degenerate to first come first serve

parsed.stem: If the time-slice used in the round-robin scheduling policy is more than the maximum time required to execute any process, then the policy will

options.text: degenerate to first come first serve

sourceQuestion:
A critical region is

A. One which is enclosed by a pair of P and V operations on semaphores.
B. A program segment that has not been proved bug-free.
C. A program segment that often causes unexpected system crashes.
D. A program segment where shared resources are accessed.
sourceAnswer: D. A program segment where shared resources are accessed.

parsed.stem: A critical region is

options.text: One which is enclosed by a pair of P and V operations on semaphores.
A program segment that has not been proved bug-free.
A program segment that often causes unexpected system crashes.
A program segment where shared resources are accessed.

sourceAnswer: C. The solution violates progress — if turn != i and flag[j] is false, P_i loops forever without entering the critical section

options.text: The solution is correct and satisfies mutual exclusion and progress
The solution violates mutual exclusion — both processes can enter the critical section simultaneously
The solution violates progress — if turn != i and flag[j] is false, P_i loops forever without entering the critical section
The solution violates bounded waiting — a process can be indefinitely postponed

sourceQuestion:
Match the pairs:

Group 1:
(a) Critical region
(b) Wait/Signal
(c) Working Set
(d) Deadlock

Group 2:
(p) Hoare's monitor
(q) Mutual exclusion
(r) Principle of locality
(s) Circular Wait

A. a-q, b-p, c-r, d-s
B. a-p, b-q, c-s, d-r
C. a-q, b-r, c-p, d-s
D. a-r, b-p, c-q, d-s

parsed.stem:
Match the pairs:

Group 1:
(a) Critical region
(b) Wait/Signal
(c) Working Set
(d) Deadlock

Group 2:
(p) Hoare's monitor
(q) Mutual exclusion
(r) Principle of locality
(s) Circular Wait

sourceAnswer: A. Yes, mutual exclusion is guaranteed because each process checks flag[j] and flag[k] before entering

options.text: Yes, mutual exclusion is guaranteed because each process checks flag[j] and flag[k] before entering
No, mutual exclusion can be violated when two processes simultaneously pass their respective flag checks
Yes, the turn variable ensures only one process enters at a time
No, the flag mechanism with three processes creates race conditions

sourceAnswer: C. Yes, mutual exclusion is ensured; but starvation is possible — a waiting process may never enter the critical section

options.text: Does the scheme ensure mutual exclusion in the critical section? Briefly explain.

Is there a situation in which a waiting process can never enter the critical section? If so, explain and suggest modifications to the code to solve this problem. A. Yes, the scheme ensures mutual exclusion B. No starvation can occur in this scheme
Yes, mutual exclusion is ensured; but starvation is possible — a waiting process may never enter the critical section
No, mutual exclusion is NOT guaranteed

sourceQuestion:
Write a concurrent program using parbegin-parend and semaphores to represent the precedence constraints of the statements S_1 to S_n, as shown in the figure below.

The precedence graph (from the figure) shows the following dependency structure:
  S_1 -> S_2
  S_1 -> S_3
  S_2 -> S_4
  S_3 -> S_4
  S_4 -> S_5
  S_4 -> S_6
  S_5 -> S_7
  S_6 -> S_7

(Diamond/fork-join shaped DAG: S1 at top, S2 and S3 in parallel, S4 as join, then S5 and S6 in parallel, S7 as final join.)

Which of the following correctly captures the concurrent program with semaphores?

A. parbegin S1; S2; S3; S4; S5; S6; S7 parend
B. S1; parbegin S2; S3 parend; S4; parbegin S5; S6 parend; S7
C. S1; parbegin S2; S3 parend; parbegin S4; S5; S6 parend; S7
D. parbegin S1; parbegin S2; S3 parend; S4 parend; S7
sourceAnswer: B. S1; parbegin S2; S3 parend; S4; parbegin S5; S6 parend; S7

parsed.stem:
Write a concurrent program using parbegin-parend and semaphores to represent the precedence constraints of the statements S_1 to S_n, as shown in the figure below.

The precedence graph (from the figure) shows the following dependency structure:
  S_1 -> S_2
  S_1 -> S_3
  S_2 -> S_4
  S_3 -> S_4
  S_4 -> S_5
  S_4 -> S_6
  S_5 -> S_7
  S_6 -> S_7

(Diamond/fork-join shaped DAG: S1 at top, S2 and S3 in parallel, S4 as join, then S5 and S6 in parallel, S7 as final join.)

Which of the following correctly captures the concurrent program with semaphores?

options.text: parbegin S1; S2; S3; S4; S5; S6; S7 parend
S1; parbegin S2; S3 parend; S4; parbegin S5; S6 parend; S7
S1; parbegin S2; S3 parend; parbegin S4; S5; S6 parend; S7
parbegin S1; parbegin S2; S3 parend; S4 parend; S7
S1 must precede S2, S3, S4
S6 requires both S3 and S2 to complete
S8 requires S6 and S7 to complete
